verses.commentary: The creation blessing is renewed. Genesis 1:28 echoes again. Noah is a new Adam. Humanity gets a fresh start.
Relationship with animals changes. Original harmony (Genesis 1:29-30) is replaced with fear and dread. Human dominion becomes more forceful. The fall's effects persist in the new world.
Permission to eat meat is granted. The vegetarian ideal of Eden gives way to carnivory. This is concession to post-fall reality, not the original design.

One restriction: no blood consumption. Blood represents life (the nephesh, נֶפֶשׁ, the life-force). This law predates Moses and reappears in Acts 15:20 for gentile believers. Respect for life is paramount.
Life is sacred. God will hold murderers accountable, whether animal or human. Human life bears God's image and must be protected.

Capital punishment is instituted. The basis is the image of God (tselem Elohim, צֶלֶם אֱלֹהִים). To kill a human is to attack God's image. This is the foundation of human dignity and the justice system.
The blessing is repeated for emphasis. Fill the earth. God's purpose for humanity continues. Despite the flood, the mission remains.
Covenant (berit, בְּרִית) is formalized. God initiated it in 6:18; now he establishes it. This is the Noahic Covenant, universal in scope.
The covenant includes animals. God's care extends to all creation. This is an ecological covenant.
God's unilateral promise. No conditions on humanity. God commits himself regardless of human behavior. Never again echoes 8:21. This is grace.
A sign (ot, אוֹת) will seal the covenant. Signs make invisible promises visible. This covenant is perpetual, for all generations.
The rainbow appears. This natural phenomenon becomes a covenant sign. Every rainbow is a reminder of God's promise.
God will remember. The rainbow triggers divine memory. God binds himself to his word. The promise is permanent.
Everlasting covenant (berit olam, בְּרִית עוֹלָם). This covenant has no end. The sign functions for God, not just humans. God sees the rainbow and remembers.
Conclusion of the covenant ceremony. The promise is sealed. The rainbow stands as eternal witness.
The narrative shifts. The three sons are named. The parenthetical note about Ham foreshadows what follows.
All humanity descends from these three. The nations of the world trace back to this moment. Unity before diversity.
Noah returns to agriculture. He plants a vineyard, the first mention of viticulture. It seems innocent but leads to trouble.
The flood's hero falls. Drunkenness and nakedness. Noah's righteousness doesn't mean perfection. Even the saved remain sinners. Vulnerability and shame return.
Ham sees and tells. The text suggests voyeurism and mockery rather than accidental discovery. Dishonoring parents is serious in biblical culture. The mention of Canaan again foreshadows the curse.
Contrast with Ham. Shem and Japheth show respect and honor. They cover their father's shame without looking. Walking backward preserves dignity.
Noah learns of Ham's dishonor. The phrase 'what his youngest son had done' suggests something more than just looking, perhaps mockery or further dishonor.
The curse falls not on Ham but on Canaan, Ham's son. This reflects the principle that consequences of sin affect descendants. This foreshadows Israel's later conquest of Canaan.
Blessing on Shem. The LORD is called the God of Shem, indicating a special relationship. Shem's line will carry the promise forward to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. The Semitic peoples include Israel.

Japheth receives blessing too. Extension of territory and dwelling with Shem. The gentile nations (Japheth) will one day share in Shem's blessing. This hints at the inclusion of gentiles in God's plan.
Noah's long life continues after the flood. He witnesses the new world for 350 years.
The drumbeat returns. Even Noah, the flood survivor, dies. Mortality persists. The flood cleanses the world but does not eliminate death. A deeper salvation is needed.
